name: orchard graft bundle
shortDescription: A tied bundle of young grafts cut from proven orchard trees, wrapped in damp cloth so the living wood keeps its promise. Garden stewards and patient landowners buy them for future harvests rather than present comfort, which makes them slow stock but dependable stock.
flavorText: “A graft is a wager that next year will still arrive.”

name: clover feed sack
shortDescription: A full sack of sweet clover feed, green-smelling and soft enough for picky stable animals. It sells best where horses are worked hard, cows are kept indoors, or caravan masters want animals calm before a long road.
flavorText: “Feed the beast well and it may forgive the road.”

name: sunpear
shortDescription: A warm-gold pear with thin skin and fragrant flesh, prized for its bright flavor and quick spoilage. It is easy to sell in the morning market and foolish to store too long unless the buyer enjoys paying for perfume instead of fruit.
flavorText: “Best eaten before the sun remembers it is fruit.”

name: moonplum
shortDescription: A dark, glossy plum with pale flesh and a sweet-tart bite that stains fingers like ink. Noble kitchens love its color, while taverns buy bruised lots cheaply for cordials, sauces, and pies.
flavorText: “A moonplum keeps night under its skin.”

name: saltmelon
shortDescription: A heavy melon with crisp flesh and a faint mineral tang, popular in hot ports and dry inland markets alike. Sliced fresh it cools workers quickly; packed in straw, it survives travel better than its watery look suggests.
flavorText: “Sweet enough for children, salty enough for sailors.”

name: ruby carrot
shortDescription: A deep red carrot with firm flesh and an earthy sweetness that cooks into a jewel-bright glaze. Market cooks prize it for color as much as flavor, because a plain stew sells better when it looks wealthy.
flavorText: “The field grew it humble. The pot makes it proud.”

name: lantern squash
shortDescription: A round orange squash with a thick rind, mellow flesh, and a glow-like color when cut open. It stores through cold weeks and sells well to inns that need one honest vegetable to carry many cheap meals.
flavorText: “A pantry lantern for the dark end of winter.”

name: glassberry basket
shortDescription: A wicker basket filled with translucent berries so delicate they seem more blown than grown. They bruise at a hard word, but their strange shine makes them valuable to confectioners, alchemists, and hosts eager to impress guests.
flavorText: “Pretty fruit demands gentle hands and bold prices.”

name: stormcap mushroom
shortDescription: A broad-capped mushroom with dusky blue edges and a scent like wet stone after thunder. Cooks use it sparingly for depth, while hedge mages argue over whether it remembers lightning or merely tastes dramatic.
flavorText: “Never trust a mushroom named after weather.”

name: pepperleaf bundle
shortDescription: A fresh bundle of sharp pepperleaf, tied with reed string and still carrying a garden snap. It wakes up flat stews, covers tired meat, and gives cheap food the confidence of something imported.
flavorText: “A leaf can shout louder than a spice jar.”

name: pork shoulder
shortDescription: A generous cut of pork shoulder, marbled for slow roasting and forgiving enough for busy kitchens. It is the sort of practical meat that feeds laborers, guards, and festival crowds without needing noble attention.
flavorText: “Low heat, long patience, full tables.”

name: beef rib slab
shortDescription: A heavy slab of beef ribs with rich fat and enough bone to promise a serious meal. Butchers like its weight, innkeepers like its smell over coals, and hungry travelers like anything that arrives still sizzling.
flavorText: “Some cuts are measured by coin. This one is measured by silence at the table.”

name: mutton haunch
shortDescription: A sturdy haunch of mutton with a strong, honest flavor that herbs can guide but not hide. Rural buyers know its worth immediately; city buyers need reminding that good meat is not always delicate.
flavorText: “Old pasture, sharp knife, full belly.”

name: pepper sausage coil
shortDescription: A coiled sausage packed with pepper, salt, and enough fat to survive a hard day’s travel. It sells well to caravans because it is easy to slice, easy to cook, and easier still to smell from across camp.
flavorText: “A rope of meat for roads that do not serve supper.”

name: salt cured bacon
shortDescription: A slab of salt cured bacon, firm with preservation and rich enough to flavor a whole pot from a thin shaving. It is common stock, but never dull stock, because kitchens run out of bacon before they admit they need more.
flavorText: “Salt keeps it safe. Smoke makes it persuasive.”

name: rendered tallow jar
shortDescription: A sealed jar of pale rendered tallow used for cooking, candles, leather work, and a dozen ungentle chores. It is not glamorous merchandise, but practical buyers respect anything that solves problems quietly.
flavorText: “Grease is never noble, but it is often necessary.”

name: butcher twine
shortDescription: A tight spool of strong butcher twine, clean, rough, and ready for trussing roasts or tying neat market bundles. It disappears quickly in busy shops, which makes it the kind of small supply that steady merchants learn to keep near.
flavorText: “The string earns no praise, yet the roast falls apart without it.”

name: silverfin fish
shortDescription: A bright silverfin fish with clean scales and firm flesh that cooks quickly over flame. Its shine helps sell it from the stall, but the real value is in freshness; one warm afternoon can turn profit into apology.
flavorText: “A fish is worth the most while it still looks offended.”

name: lanternfish
shortDescription: A strange little lanternfish with pale spots that seem to glow in dim light. Dockside cooks treat it as a novelty, while scholars and alchemists pay extra for specimens that have not been dropped, dried, or lied about.
flavorText: “Some things from deep water bring their own lamps.”

name: tide eel
shortDescription: A slick tide eel coiled in a wet reed basket, difficult to hold and excellent in strong broths. It sells best to buyers who already know what they are doing, since beginners mostly purchase panic.
flavorText: “The eel is fresh if the merchant is nervous.”

name: reef crab
shortDescription: A hard-shelled reef crab with bright claws and sweet meat hidden behind stubborn armor. Coastal taverns buy them by the basket, and inland nobles buy them by the story.
flavorText: “Anything this difficult to open must be worth eating.”

name: moon oyster
shortDescription: A pale moon oyster with a smooth shell and a clean briny scent. It is delicate, expensive, and easy to ruin, which means every sale depends on speed, trust, and a buyer who understands risk.
flavorText: “The sea keeps its pearls. It sells the rest to fools and gourmets.”

name: salted cod barrel
shortDescription: A small barrel packed with salted cod, layered tight enough to survive months of road, ship, and suspicion. It is not fresh, not pretty, and not cheap when supplies run thin far from the coast.
flavorText: “Salted fish is what the sea becomes when merchants get organized.”

name: fish roe jar
shortDescription: A sealed jar of fish roe, glossy with brine and valued in kitchens that enjoy charging extra for tiny things. It must be kept cool, handled gently, and described carefully to buyers who confuse rarity with luxury.
flavorText: “A spoonful for the rich, a month’s argument for the poor.”

name: squid ink flask
shortDescription: A stoppered flask of deep black squid ink used for sauces, dyes, and certain dramatic letters. Cooks want its sea-dark flavor; scribes want its color; pranksters are not allowed near it twice.
flavorText: “Black enough to season a dish or ruin a contract.”

name: honey ale keg
shortDescription: A snug keg of honey ale, sweet at first and warm in the throat after. It travels better than fancier wines, making it welcome at roadside inns, harvest suppers, and negotiations that need softening.
flavorText: “Honey makes the ale polite. The ale does the rest.”

name: pear wine bottle
shortDescription: A clear bottle of pear wine with a light golden color and a gentle orchard scent. It is easy to underestimate, which is why careful hosts serve it early and careless guests remember it late.
flavorText: “A quiet wine with a cheerful habit of sneaking up.”

name: berry cordial
shortDescription: A dark berry cordial, sweet, strong, and thick enough to stain the glass. Apothecaries sell it as comfort, taverns sell it as celebration, and grandmothers insist it cures nearly everything.
flavorText: “Medicine, dessert, and excuse in one bottle.”

name: spiced tea tin
shortDescription: A tin of dry tea blended with warming spices, sealed against damp and kitchen gossip. It sells well in cold months and better during negotiations, because a hot cup gives everyone time to pretend they are reasonable.
flavorText: “Steam has settled more arguments than steel.”

name: mintwater flask
shortDescription: A cool flask of mintwater, clean-tasting and favored by travelers crossing hot roads or crowded streets. It is simple refreshment, but good merchants know thirst pays quickly and asks few questions.
flavorText: “Fresh breath, clear head, lighter purse.”

name: pewter tankard
shortDescription: A sturdy pewter tankard with a dull shine, a comfortable handle, and small dents from older tables. It lacks noble grace, but it holds drink honestly and survives tavern life better than prettier cups.
flavorText: “A tankard should outlast the song that empties it.”

name: blueglass cup
shortDescription: A blueglass cup with cool color and a pleasing weight, fine enough for a respectable table without becoming a robbery invitation. Light through it turns even plain water into something worth presenting.
flavorText: “Some glassware improves the drink before it is poured.”

name: Tidefall saffron
shortDescription: A tiny sealed packet of Tidefall saffron, red-gold threads worth more than their weight suggests. Cooks use it carefully, dyers use it reverently, and merchants count it twice before trusting the ledger.
flavorText: “The richest color in the smallest parcel.”

name: smoked salt jar
shortDescription: A jar of coarse smoked salt, gray-white crystals carrying the scent of old embers. It rescues bland food, flatters preserved meat, and lets a humble kitchen borrow the memory of a hearth.
flavorText: “When the fire is gone, the salt remembers.”

name: star salt jar
shortDescription: A small jar of star salt, bright-flecked and faintly sparkling under lamplight. Superstitious cooks save it for feast dishes, while alchemists insist the glitter is either mineral, magical, or priced correctly.
flavorText: “A pinch for luck, and another for the customer watching.”

name: cinnamon bark roll
shortDescription: A rolled strip of cinnamon bark with warm fragrance locked inside its curled edge. It is light to carry, easy to overcharge for, and impossible to hide from a good-nosed buyer.
flavorText: “A spice that announces itself before the merchant does.”

name: vanilla pod tube
shortDescription: A slim tube holding fragrant vanilla pods, protected from damp, theft, and careless fingers. Bakers treat it like treasure because a little turns plain cream, cakes, and sauces into memory.
flavorText: “Sweetness is cheaper than nostalgia. Vanilla sells both.”

name: berry jam jar
shortDescription: A stout jar of berry jam, deep red and thick with seeds, sugar, and summer. It is humble stock until winter arrives, when preserved fruit begins speaking with a richer voice.
flavorText: “A spoonful of warm months sealed against the cold.”

name: pickled onion jar
shortDescription: A jar of pickled onions, sharp, bright, and ready to wake up bread, cheese, or tired salted meat. Caravan cooks favor it because sourness travels well and hides many sins.
flavorText: “The onion wept first. Now it makes everyone else blink.”

name: sailcloth roll
shortDescription: A heavy roll of sailcloth, tightly woven and tough enough for wind, rain, tents, sacks, and repairs. Shipwrights buy it by need, travelers by hope, and quartermasters by the uncomfortable dozen.
flavorText: “Canvas that has not yet chosen a sea.”

name: blue brocade panel
shortDescription: A panel of blue brocade with raised patterns that catch the light like quiet waves. Tailors and upholsterers buy it for show pieces, since a little rich cloth can make plain wood or simple sleeves look important.
flavorText: “Wealth is often just thread standing slightly higher.”

name: sea silk scarf
shortDescription: A soft sea silk scarf with a faint shimmer and a cool touch against the skin. It is delicate enough for noble wardrobes but practical merchants sell the story first and the cloth second.
flavorText: “The sea gave up no pearls for this, only patience.”

name: gold thread spool
shortDescription: A small spool of gold thread, bright, costly, and easy to lose on a cluttered workbench. Embroiderers use it to turn crests, hems, and ceremonial cloth into things people bow toward.
flavorText: “One thin line between garment and inheritance.”

name: bone needle case
shortDescription: A narrow bone case holding fine needles protected from rust, bending, and wandering hands. Seamstresses, surgeons, and traveling tailors all value it for the same reason: sharp tools should not be loose in a bag.
flavorText: “The case is plain because the needles do the delicate work.”

name: pattern paper roll
shortDescription: A roll of marked pattern paper, covered with measurements, folds, and tailor’s shorthand. To the untrained it looks like waste; to a cutter it is the beginning of a coat that fits before it exists.
flavorText: “Cloth obeys better when paper has argued first.”

name: cured hide
shortDescription: A cured hide scraped, stretched, and dried for future cutting. It smells of tannin and smoke, but saddlers and armorers see boots, straps, patches, and profit in every broad piece.
flavorText: “Beast, field, knife, and trade all flattened into one sheet.”

name: waxed boot leather
shortDescription: A stack of waxed boot leather, darkened and treated to fight rain, mud, and long roads. Cobblers prefer it for customers who cannot afford dainty footwear because the road has no respect for fashion.
flavorText: “Good boots begin before the first stitch.”

name: dyed strap bundle
shortDescription: A bundle of dyed leather straps in useful lengths, sorted for harnesses, packs, buckles, and repairs. The colors help them sell, but their true value appears when something important snaps on the road.
flavorText: “A bright strap is still judged by what it holds.”

name: saddle pad
shortDescription: A thick saddle pad stuffed and stitched to spare both rider and beast. It is not the grandest part of a mount’s kit, but anyone who has ridden a full day understands its quiet importance.
flavorText: “Comfort is cheapest before the journey begins.”

name: shell button blanks
shortDescription: A small pouch of shell button blanks, smooth, pale, and ready for drilling and polishing. Button makers prize their shimmer because even plain clothing gains a little sea-born vanity when fastened properly.
flavorText: “Tiny moons for shirts that dream above their station.”
